rulings.Case Title: SINGLER-ERNSTER, INC. VS. ROUND TABLE FRANCHISE CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Singler-Ernster, Inc.'S Petition To Vacate Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 22, 2014, Line 5, PETITIONER SINGLER-ERNSTER, INC.'s Petition To Vacate Arbitration Award. Denied. Petitioner has not established that the arbitrator exceeded her powers. In Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, 941, the Court explicitly states that it limited its review to the issue of to what extent Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 16600 prohibits employee noncompetition agreements. Therefore, the arbitrator's ruling that the in-term, non-competition provision of the franchise agreement did not violate Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 16600 was not a clear violation of public policy or in manifest disregard of the law. Further, Petitioner has not established that the entire contractual transaction was illegal; rather, Petitioner only challenges a single provision of the contract. (See Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal.4th 1, 32, "[moving party] challenges but a single provision of the overall employment contract¿.Loving & Evans [does not] authorize[] judicial review of his claim.") Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: LIDIA C BORRAYO VS. G. JAMES AVERY II, M.D. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 22, 2014, Line 8, DEFENDANT G. AVERY, M.D.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The court denies defendant's CCP 170.6 challenge as untimely. The court's register of actions (docket) indicates that defendant filed a CCP 170.6 challenge to Judge Goldsmith on August 18, 2014. The court has yet to receive a courtesy copy of the challenge. The challenge appears to be untimely under the 10 day/5 day rule. See CCP 170.6(a)(2) ["If the judge, other than a judge assigned to the case for all purposes, court commissioner, or referee assigned to, or who is scheduled to try, the cause or hear the matter is known at least 10 days before the date set for trial or hearing, the motion shall be made at least 5 days before that date."]. In People v. Sup. Crt. (1993) 4 Cal.4th 1164, 1180-1184, the court explains that where a judge regularly sits in a department and his identity is known or discoverable upon reasonable inquiry at least 10 days before the hearing, the challenge must be made at least 5 days before the hearing date. In the instant matter, Judge Goldsmith regularly sits in the law and motion department and it appears that the challenge is one day late. See CCP 12c(a) [counting days]. With regard to the merits, the court requires a hearing. Plaintiff is to be prepared to inform the court whether her expert is licensed to practice medicine in any state in the United States. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: VASU D. ARORA VS. ARNOLD LAUB et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Vacate And Set Aside Order Of 9/29/14, Being A Fraud On The Court By The Defts Who Thereby Lacked Jurisidction; And Because Of Mistake, Inadvertence, Surprise And Excusable Neglect Under Ccp 473(B)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 22, 2014, Line 14, PLAINTIFF VASU ARORA's Motion To Vacate And Set Aside Order Of 9/29/14, Being A Fraud On The Court By The Defendants Who Thereby Lacked Jurisidction; And Because Of Mistake, Inadvertence, Surprise And Excusable Neglect Under Ccp 473(B). Denied. The motion is untimely under CCP § 1005(b). The motion was filed and served on August 4. Moving party only provided 14 court days notice and did not account for the five additional calendar days required for mailing. Additionally, the motion is denied on the merits. Plaintiff does not provide an explanation as to why he did not file an opposition to the motion to dismiss or attend the July 29, 2014 hearing. The proofs of service filed by Defendants establish that Plaintiff was notified about the July 29, 2014 hearing date via email and mail. (Mark R. Meyer Declaration, Ex. A.) rulings.Case Title: ANGELICA PEREZ VS. KWUN SHIU YAN FUNG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Angelica Perez'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, August 22, 2014, Line 1, Plaintiff Angelica Perez'S Motion To Quash Subpoenas. Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Judge Pro Tem has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to quash is granted as to all subpoenas. The subpoenas were served by the defendant to obtain plaintiff's personnel and medical records, generally speaking. However, this is an action for violation of the San Francisco ordinance regarding owner move-in evictions. The plaintiff has disclaimed any damages for loss of wages or personal injury, other than emotional distress damages relating to the eviction itself. The records sought by defendant are protected by a constitutional right to privacy. Defendant must come forward with a compelling reason why these documents are relevant to the case and the information cannot be obtained by less intrusive means. None of the arguments offered by Defendant are persuasive. It is not relevant whether plaintiff listed the apartment from which she was evicted as her primary residence, or listed her father as an occupant. The medical records are not sufficiently germane to plaintiff's alleged injury to overcome their protected status. Finally, Defendant identifies none of her more than twenty affirmative defenses to which the subpoenaed information would relate. Accordingly, the Court quashes the subpoenas. Plaintiff also seeks sanctions for the time spent bringing the motions to quash. Plaintiff points out that she sought to meet and confer over the subpoenas, but received no response, a fact which Defendant does not dispute. The Court finds that sanctions are warranted as to the subpoenas for personnel records, for which there is no substantial justification and as to which Defendant did not meet and confer. The subpoenas for medical records have a more arguably legitimate basis, although not one ultimately supported by the arguments Defendant has presented. Accordingly, the Court grants the motion for sanctions in the amount of $700. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CROSS CREEK SYSTEMS VS. SYMPHONY COMMERCE, INC. FKA SNEAKPEEQ, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 22, 2014, Line 18, DEFENDANT SYMPHONY COMMERCE, INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. The court overrules the demurrer in its entirety. The contract is not an "agreement to agree." Paragraphs 6-11 of the complaint clearly allege a breach of contract cause of action and plaintiff even attaches a copy of the parties' written agreement. Defendant does not show that plaintiff's construction of the contract is erroneous. A demurrer to a cause of action for breach of contract admits not only the contents of the contract, but also any pleaded meaning that the instrument is reasonably susceptible. See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc., (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239 ("While plaintiffs' interpretation of the contract may prove invalid, it [is] improper to resolve the issue against her solely on her own pleading. 'In ruling on a demurrer, the likelihood that the pleading will be able to prove his allegations is not the question.'"). Defendant argues that plaintiff needs to plead more facts regarding how plaintiff performed the contract, e.g., vetting, interviewing. CCP 457 expressly provides that a party alleging a breach of contract can plead its performance generally. The common counts cause of action is well pleaded and a factual issue remains whether plaintiff performed the required services. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: MARSHALL HAYNER VS. NATHANIEL LANDS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay The Proceedings Under California Civil Procedure Code Section 1281; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, August 22, 2014, Line 19, DEFENDANTS NATHANIEL LANDS, WILLIAM COTTON and QUICKCOIN, INC.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay The Proceedings Under California Civil Procedure Code Section 1281; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities. The motion to compel arbitration is granted and the court stays this action pursuant to CCP 1281.4. Defendants sufficiently establish the arbitration agreement and plaintiff does not dispute the existence of the agreement. See Condee v. Longwood Mgmt. Corp. (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 215. The arbitration clause is broad enough to apply to all of the cited disputes between the parties. "[A]ny doubts concerning the scope of arbitrable issues should be resolved in favor of arbitration." Cronus Investments, Inc. v. Concierge Servs. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 376, 384. The declaration of defendants' counsel (Mr. Yurasek) at par. 5 indicates that defendants demanded arbitration in compliance with CCP 1281.2. Lastly, plaintiff does not sufficiently show that he was fraudulently induced into agreeing to arbitrate his disputes. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EHG)
